title: Personal Calorie Tracker with Food Photo Upload & Step Tracking

requirements.text: Create a backend data model and actor to store user food log entries per day. Each entry must include: date (Text), food name (Text), calorie estimate (Nat), and an image reference (Text). Provide functions to add a food entry, retrieve all entries for a given date, and retrieve a list of all distinct logged dates.

quotes: uploading pictures of food it can tell how much calories intake will be there by that food and add it to my daily consumption
keeps a record of my daily intake

acceptanceCriteria: addFoodEntry(date, foodName, calories, imageRef) stores a new entry
getFoodEntriesByDate(date) returns all entries for that date
getAllLoggedDates() returns a deduplicated list of dates that have entries
Data persists across canister upgrades via stable storage

requirements.text: Create a backend data model and functions to store daily step count records. Each record includes: date (Text) and step count (Nat). Provide functions to set/update steps for a date and retrieve steps for a given date. Calculate calories burned from steps using the standard approximation of 0.04 kcal per step.

quotes: enable feature of recording foot steps which tells how much calories it reduces

acceptanceCriteria: setStepsForDate(date, steps) stores or updates the step count for that date
getStepsForDate(date) returns the step count and computed calories burned
Calories burned calculation uses 0.04 kcal/step
Step data persists across canister upgrades

requirements.text: Create a backend function to return a full daily summary for a given date, including: total calories consumed (sum of all food entries), total steps taken, total calories burned from steps, and net calories (consumed minus burned).

quotes: tell me how much calories i took in a day
keeps a record of my daily intake

acceptanceCriteria: getDailySummary(date) returns totalCaloriesIn, totalSteps, caloriesBurned, and netCalories
Correctly sums all food entries for the date
Net calories = totalCaloriesIn - caloriesBurned

requirements.text: Build a Food Upload screen where the user can upload or select a photo of food. The app must read the image, display a preview, and perform a client-side heuristic analysis of the image filename and basic color/size signals to produce a plausible calorie estimate range. Present the estimated calories and a suggested food name to the user, who can confirm or manually adjust both values before saving the entry to the backend for the current date.

quotes: uploading pictures of food it can tell how much calories intake will be there by that food and add it to my daily consumption

acceptanceCriteria: User can pick or drag-and-drop a food photo
A food name and calorie estimate are displayed after upload
User can edit the food name and calorie value before confirming
Confirming calls addFoodEntry on the backend and shows a success message
Image preview is shown throughout the flow

requirements.text: Build a Daily Dashboard screen (defaulting to today's date) showing: a date selector to switch days, a list of all food entries logged for that day (food name, calories, thumbnail), total calories consumed, a step count input where the user can log their steps for the day, total calories burned from steps, and a net calorie balance indicator (calories in minus calories out). Display a motivational message indicating whether the user is in a calorie deficit or surplus.

quotes: tell me how much calories i took in a day
enable feature of recording foot steps which tells how much calories it reduces
keep record of calories intake so that i can work on reducing my fat in body

acceptanceCriteria: Dashboard loads today's data by default
Date picker allows viewing any previously logged day
Food entries list shows food name and calories for the selected date
Step count input saves to the backend on submit
Total calories in, calories burned, and net calories are displayed clearly
A calorie deficit is highlighted positively (green); surplus is highlighted as a warning (amber/red)

requirements.text: Build a History Log screen that lists all dates that have logged food entries or step data. For each date, show the total calories consumed, total calories burned, and net calories. Tapping or clicking a date navigates to that date's Daily Dashboard. Display a summary trend (e.g., average daily net calories across all logged days).

quotes: keeps a record of my daily intake
keep record of calories intake so that i can work on reducing my fat in body

acceptanceCriteria: History screen lists all days that have at least one entry
Each row shows date, calories in, calories burned, net calories
Clicking a date row navigates to the Dashboard for that date
Average daily net calorie figure is shown at the top of the history list

requirements.text: Design and apply a cohesive visual theme across the entire app. Use a fresh, health-and-wellness-inspired aesthetic: clean white and soft green tones with warm orange accents, rounded cards, clear typography with strong hierarchy, and an airy layout. The design should feel motivating and approachable for a daily fat-loss tracking use case.

quotes: keep record of calories intake so that i can work on reducing my fat in body

acceptanceCriteria: Consistent color palette (soft greens, white backgrounds, warm orange CTAs) applied throughout
All screens use rounded card components and clear section headings
Navigation between Food Upload, Dashboard, and History is intuitive and always accessible
App feels visually cohesive and polished on both mobile and desktop viewport widths

constraints: No external AI/ML API integrations — calorie estimation must be fully client-side or rule-based
All data must be stored in the Motoko backend canister using stable storage
Do not edit any files listed under frontend.immutablePaths
Single Motoko actor for all backend logic
nonGoals: GPS or real-time step counting via device sensors
Integration with wearables or external fitness APIs
Nutritional breakdown beyond calorie counts (macros, vitamins, etc.)

required: App logo mark: a stylized plate with a leaf and small flame icon, fresh and minimal (logo-mark.dim_256x256.png)
Onboarding hero illustration: a top-down flat-lay of colorful healthy foods on a white surface with warm tones (hero-food.dim_1200x400.png)
